ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for butter roti specifically; A000E (bread and similar products) used as closest defensible category anchor for a whole-wheat flatbread with added butter/ghee. The specific description 'flatbread, whole wheat, with added fat' was removed as it is not part of the official FoodEx2 description for A000E.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for butter roti; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard butter roti weighs approximately 100 g and represents a typical single-piece serving as commonly portioned in Singapore Indian restaurants and hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Chapati / Roti (whole wheat, cooked) with butter adaptation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN data for whole-wheat chapati/roti used as the primary nutritional anchor. Butter contribution (approximately 10–12 g butter per 100 g finished roti) was calculated using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for butter (salted), FDC ID 173410, to derive fat, saturated fat, cholesterol, and energy uplift. Final per-100g values represent the composite cooked product as served with butter/ghee. Values are consistent with the input record and cross-validated against Singapore HPB Foodo database entries for similar Indian flatbreads.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 62 derived from University of Sydney GI Database entry for whole wheat chapati / roti (GI range 52–70, representative value 62, medium category). Butter roti is a whole-wheat chapati with added butter/ghee; the fat addition modestly lowers the effective GI relative to plain chapati by slowing gastric emptying, but the whole-wheat base GI of 62 is used as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (62 × 35.0) / 100 = 21.7 per 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 250 mg/100 g with elevated saturated fat 7.0 g/100 g from butter and ghee
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 250 mg/100 g is borderline for hypertension management; patients should limit to one roti per meal, request reduced butter application, and pair with a potassium-rich accompaniment such as dal or vegetable curry to partially offset sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate 35 g/100 g at medium GI 62 (GL 21.7), partially mitigated by dietary fibre 3.5 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Whole wheat fibre at 3.5 g/100 g slows glucose absorption and lowers the glycaemic response relative to refined flour roti; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one roti per meal, request minimal butter application to reduce saturated fat that may worsen insulin resistance, and pair with a protein-rich dal to further moderate postprandial glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 7.0 g/100 g and trans fat 0.3 g/100 g from butter and ghee raise LDL-cholesterol risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 7.0 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.3 g/100 g from butter and ghee are clinically significant for cardiovascular disease; patients should request a minimal or no-butter preparation, substitute with a small amount of extra-virgin olive oil if possible, and rely on the whole-wheat fibre base for cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 7.0 g/100 g from butter and ghee exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA pro-inflammatory threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saturated fat at 7.0 g/100 g from butter and ghee exceeds the RA threshold and promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity; patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid butter/ghee application and substitute with a small amount of EVOO to retain the anti-inflammatory benefit of the whole-wheat fibre base.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butter Roti is a traditional Indian whole-wheat flatbread widely consumed across India and in Singapore's Indian hawker and restaurant culture. It is prepared by kneading whole wheat flour (atta) with water and salt into a soft dough, rolling it into a thin round disc, and cooking it on a hot tawa (cast-iron griddle) until lightly browned and puffed. The cooked roti is then generously brushed with butter or ghee before serving, giving it a rich, slightly nutty flavour and a soft, pliable texture with lightly charred spots. Per 100 g, butter roti provides approximately 280 kcal, 7.5 g protein, 12.0 g fat (of which 7.0 g is saturated fat from butter/ghee), 35.0 g carbohydrate, 3.5 g dietary fibre, and 250 mg sodium. The whole-wheat base contributes meaningful dietary fibre that moderates the glycaemic response (GI ~62, medium category), making it a nutritionally superior choice compared to refined-flour roti or naan. Clinically, the elevated saturated fat from butter and ghee is a concern for cardiovascular disease, rheumatoid arthritis, and insulin resistance in Type 2 Diabetes management. Butter roti is halal and vegetarian, and is commonly served as a staple accompaniment to dal, curry, or sabzi in Indian meals. In Singapore, it is a popular breakfast and lunch item at Indian Muslim and South Indian eateries.
common_user_queries: How many calories in butter roti?
Is butter roti good for diabetics?
Difference between butter roti and plain roti
Can I eat butter roti with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with ICMR-NIN whole-wheat chapati data adapted for butter/ghee addition and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central butter entries. No exact 'butter roti' entry exists in a single approved primary source; values represent a defensible composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for whole-wheat chapati. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite adaptation rather than a single exact source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the composite butter roti preparation is not separately reported in ICMR-NIN or Singapore HPB Foodo database entries as of 2024; USDA FoodData Central butter data provides saturated fat but full MUFA/PUFA split for the composite dish is not available. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for the composite butter roti preparation are not fully reported in a single approved source for this specific dish. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible/trace in whole-wheat flatbread with butter; consistent with ICMR-NIN and USDA data. trans_fat_g 0.3 is present from butter (naturally occurring ruminant trans fat, primarily vaccenic acid). Ethnic tags in source input included irrelevant entries ('Butter Chicken', 'Curry', 'Naan', 'Biryani') which have been removed from cultural classification as they are separate dishes, not aliases or tags for butter roti. Source URL 'https://foodo.sg/' in input record does not directly support the nutrient values claimed; replaced with ICMR-NIN as primary source with USDA FoodData Central as secondary for butter contribution.
